sections.content: A MAYDAY RELAY is transmitted by a station that is not itself in distress but is relaying a distress message on behalf of another vessel. The relay ensures that a distress situation is made known to coast stations and other vessels that may be able to assist, even when the vessel in distress is unable to communicate effectively.

A MAYDAY RELAY should be transmitted in the following circumstances: (1) the vessel in distress is unable to transmit the distress call itself, for example due to equipment failure or because the crew is occupied with survival; (2) a distress call has been heard but remains unanswered after a reasonable interval and no coast station has acknowledged; (3) the vessel in distress has been interrupted mid-transmission and has not resumed; or (4) the vessel in distress or a coast station has specifically requested a relay.

sections.title: Who May Relay?
sections.content: Any vessel or coast station that is aware of a distress situation may transmit a MAYDAY RELAY. There is no restriction on which station initiates the relay -- the priority is ensuring that the distress is communicated.
The voice format for a MAYDAY RELAY is as follows:

rows: MAYDAY RELAY MAYDAY RELAY MAYDAY RELAY
THIS IS [own vessel name x3, own call sign, own MMSI]
Following received from [vessel in distress, its MMSI] on Channel 16
[Repeat the full MAYDAY message as received]

sections.content: When relaying, your first priority should be to contact a coast station, as coast stations have the resources and authority to coordinate search and rescue operations. If you are unable to reach a coast station, relay the MAYDAY on Channel 16 to all stations. Ensure you quote the original message as accurately as possible -- do not paraphrase or omit details, even if they seem redundant.

sections.title: Do Not Delay a Relay
sections.content: If a distress call goes unanswered, do not wait indefinitely for someone else to relay it. A delay in relaying could cost lives. If no acknowledgment is heard after a reasonable interval, transmit the relay yourself.

On DSC, a distress relay alert can also be sent. However, ships should normally relay distress by voice on Channel 16 after attempting to contact a coast station. DSC distress relay alerts transmitted by ships are intended primarily for situations where no voice communication with a coast station is possible. Coast stations use DSC relay alerts to broadcast distress information to all ships in the area.

sections.prompt: Which of the following is NOT a valid reason to transmit a MAYDAY RELAY?

options.text: The vessel in distress cannot transmit due to equipment failure
A distress call has been heard and promptly acknowledged by a coast station
The distress call has gone unanswered after a reasonable interval

sections.explanation: If a coast station has already acknowledged the distress call, there is no need to relay it. A MAYDAY RELAY is used when the distress call has not been acknowledged, when the vessel cannot transmit, or when a relay has been specifically requested.

sections.prompt: In a MAYDAY RELAY, the original distress message is enclosed between the words QUOTE and ___.
sections.explanation: The prowords QUOTE and UNQUOTE are used to clearly delimit the original distress message being relayed, distinguishing it from the relaying station's own identification and transmission.

sections.prompt: When transmitting a MAYDAY RELAY, who should you try to contact first?
sections.explanation: Your first priority when relaying a distress message should be to contact a coast station, as they have the resources and authority to coordinate search and rescue operations. If no coast station can be reached, relay to all stations on Channel 16.

points: A MAYDAY RELAY is sent when the vessel in distress cannot transmit, or when its call goes unanswered or is interrupted.
Any vessel or coast station may transmit a MAYDAY RELAY -- there is no restriction.
The format is: MAYDAY RELAY x3, THIS IS (own vessel x3, MMSI), following received from (vessel in distress, MMSI), QUOTE, repeat original message, UNQUOTE, OVER.
Contact a coast station first; if none responds, relay on Channel 16 to all stations.
Quote the original distress message exactly -- do not paraphrase or omit details.
